title: Vegetable biryani
subtitle: Layered rice and spiced vegetables, sealed and steamed.
excerpt: Basmati par-cooked with whole spice, layered with a thick spiced vegetable masala, sealed in a lidded pot and steamed for 25 minutes. The classic Hyderabadi dum technique, the vegetarian household version.
sourceNotes: The dum technique of sealed steam-cooking is documented in Indian regional food writing from the Mughal courts through to the modern home kitchen. This is the household vegetarian version, not the formal banquet style.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well in portions for up to 2 months. Defrost overnight, reheat gently in a covered pan with a splash of water.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The vegetable masala and the rice can both be cooked up to 4 hours ahead. Assemble and dum just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: An Indian technique of sealing a pot with dough or foil and cooking the contents over low heat so the steam stays trapped inside. The food cooks gently in its own moisture; the seal is broken only at the table.

content.text: Biryani works on two pots and one assembly. In the first, basmati rice is half-cooked with whole spices in salted water and drained. In the second, the vegetable masala is built up: onions fried hard, ginger and garlic in, yoghurt-marinated vegetables added, simmered down until thick and dark. Then the two go together in layers (rice, masala, rice, masala, rice on top), and the pot is sealed and steamed for 25 minutes.

The rice has to be 70 to 80 percent cooked when it goes into the layers, not fully done. It finishes cooking in the steam. The masala should be thick enough to sit in heaps, not pour: too wet and the rice goes mushy at the bottom.

items.prepNote: soaked in cold water for 30 minutes, drained
medium, peeled and cut into 2 cm cubes
peeled and cut into 1 cm rounds
trimmed and cut into 3 cm pieces

content.text: A heavy lidded casserole or Dutch oven of at least 4 litres for the dum. The lid needs to fit tightly; a sheet of foil between pan and lid seals it properly.

Bring 3 litres of water to the boil in a large saucepan. Add {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, the {{cinnamon-stick}} of cinnamon, {{cardamom-green}} of cardamom, {{cloves}} of cloves, and {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves. Drop in the soaked {{basmati-rice}} of rice. Cook for 5 to 6 minutes only: the grains should still have a hard core when bitten. Drain in a fine sieve over the sink.

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in the casserole over medium-high. Add {{onion}} of sliced onions. Cook for 12 to 14 minutes, stirring often, until deep brown and crisp at the edges. Lift two-thirds out onto kitchen paper with a slotted spoon, leaving the rest in the pan.

To the onions left in the pan, add {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{ginger-root}} of crushed ginger, and the slit green chilli. Stir for 1 minute. Add {{coriander-ground}} of coriander, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Stir for 30 seconds.

Whisk {{greek-yoghurt}} of yoghurt smooth in a separate bowl. Pour it into the pan and stir for 2 minutes; this stops it curdling. Add {{potato}} of potato, {{carrot}} of carrot, {{cauliflower}} of cauliflower, {{green-beans}} of green beans, and {{peas-frozen}} of peas. Pour in 200 ml of water. Cover and simmer for 12 to 15 minutes until the potato is tender. Uncover and bubble for 3 minutes to thicken the gravy until it's spoonable, not pourable. Stir in {{garam-masala}} of garam masala.

Spread half the masala in a single layer in the casserole. Top with half the par-cooked rice in an even layer. Scatter half the reserved crisp onions, half the {{mint}} of chopped mint, half the {{coriander}} of chopped coriander. Repeat with the rest. Drizzle the saffron milk and the {{ghee}} of melted ghee over the top.

Cover the pan with a sheet of foil, pressed down around the edges. Set the lid on top. Cook on the lowest possible heat for 25 minutes; on a gas hob, use a heat-diffuser if you have one. The rice steams in its own moisture.
Lift the pan off the heat and let it rest, sealed, for 15 minutes. Open at the table. Stir gently to mix the layers as you serve. Eat with a raita of yoghurt, cucumber, and mint.

items.symptom: Rice mushy at the bottom of the pan
items.cause: Masala too wet, or rice cooked too long before layering.
items.fix: Cook the masala until it sits in heaps with no free liquid. Par-cook the rice only 5 to 6 minutes, with a hard core still in the grain when bitten.

items.cause: Heat too high during the dum.
items.fix: Use the lowest possible setting on the hob. A heat-diffuser plate under a gas burner is a good investment for this dish.

items.symptom: Top rice dry while bottom is wet
items.fix: Press a sheet of foil over the rim before the lid goes on. The steam needs to stay trapped.

items.cause: Yoghurt added cold to a hot pan without stirring.
items.fix: Whisk the yoghurt smooth first. Stir constantly for 2 minutes as it goes in, on medium not high heat.

content.text: Biryani is the dish of weddings, celebrations, and Sunday lunches across the subcontinent. The Hyderabadi style (kacchi biryani, where raw marinated meat goes into the layers) is the most famous; the vegetable version here uses pakka technique, where the masala is fully cooked before layering. Serve with raita, a wedge of lemon, and a green chilli pickle to one side.
